title: Final Project
text:
Dataset

game_films <- read_csv("data/game_films.csv")

glimpse(game_films)

Rows: 439
Columns: 20
$ category                      <chr> "Theatrical releases", "Theatrical relea…
$ subcategory                   <chr> "English", "English", "English", "Englis…
$ title                         <chr> "Super Mario Bros.", "Double Dragon", "S…
$ director                      <chr> "Rocky Morton and Annabel Jankel", "Jame…
$ release_date                  <date> 1993-05-28, 1994-11-04, 1994-12-23, 199…
$ release_date_raw              <chr> "May 28, 1993", "November 4, 1994", "Dec…
$ air_date_raw                  <chr> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, …
$ worldwide_box_office_currency <chr> "$", "$", "$", "$", "$", "$", "$", "$", …
$ worldwide_box_office          <dbl> 38912465, 4152699, 99423521, 124741822, …
$ rotten_tomatoes               <dbl> 29, 12, 11, 47, 4, 10, 20, 36, 24, 3, 18…
$ metacritic                    <dbl> 35, 40, 34, 60, 11, 21, 33, 33, 43, 15, …
$ cinema_score                  <chr> "B+", "F", "B-", "A-", "C+", "D", "B", "…
$ distributor                   <chr> "Buena Vista Pictures Distribution", "Gr…
$ original_game_publisher       <chr> "Nintendo", "Technōs Japan", "Capcom", "…
$ budget_currency               <chr> "$", "$", "$", "$", "$", "$", "$", "$", …
$ budget_low                    <dbl> 4.20e+07, 7.80e+06, 3.50e+07, 2.00e+07, …
$ budget_high                   <dbl> 4.80e+07, 7.80e+06, 3.50e+07, 2.00e+07, …
$ domestic_box_office           <chr> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, …
$ subject                       <chr> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, …
$ network                       <chr> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, …



game_films |> 
  count(category, sort = TRUE)

# A tibble: 5 × 2
  category                         n
  <chr>                        <int>
1 Theatrical releases            246
2 Documentaries on video games    67
3 Direct-to-video                 54
4 Short films                     46
5 Television films                26


This TidyTuesday dataset, Films Based on Video Games, is scraped from the Wikipedia article List of films based on video games and curated by Georgios Karamanis. Each row is one film adaptation, with 20 variables spanning 439 films released since the early 1980s to upcoming titles.
Source: TidyTuesday, 2026-06-09, scraped from Wikipedia’s List of films based on video games.
It covers theatrical releases, direct-to-video, TV films, shorts, and documentaries, recording box office, budgets, critic and audience scores, distributors, and the original game publisher. I chose it because game-to-film adaptations have been growing greatly in the past decades, which raises questions as to how these adaptations actually do.
Question 2 will additionally merge an external video-games ratings dataset (more on that below).

title: Final Project
text:
Questions

Commercial performance. Among films adapted from video games, how does worldwide box office vary based on the publisher of the video game that was adapted into a film and the time in which it was released, and how often do these films even break even?
Game vs. film ratings. For video game franchises adapted into film, how do the films’ review scores compare to the ratings of their corresponding original games, and does this game-vs-film gap differ across publishers or different times?

title: Final Project
text:
Analysis plan

Question 1: Commercial performance

Variables used: worldwide_box_office, worldwide_box_office_currency, budget_low, budget_high, original_game_publisher, release_date.
Variables to create: release_era (decade, from release_date), profit and roi (box office to mid-budget), box_office_real (adjusting for inflation via CPI), a collapsed publisher factor (top publishers + “Other”).
Filtering: restrict to $(USD) box office for comparability (82 films); box office + budget overlap is 62 films, which I will use as the profitability sample.
Approach: box office and return on investment summarized by publisher (bar/boxplot) and by era (trend), plus a budget vs box-office scatter plot to show which adaptations actually turned a profit.
External Data merged: Annual U.S. CPI, used to convert box office amounts into inflation-adjusted dollars.



Question 2: Game vs. film ratings

Variables used (films): metacritic, rotten_tomatoes, cinema_score, original_game_publisher, release_date.
Variables to create: a franchise key applied to both tables via str_detect (e.g., all “Resident Evil” titles -> one franchise), franchise level averages of game and film scores, a rating_gap = average game score - average film score, numeric conversion of cinema_score letter grades for an audience comparison.
External Data merged: Video Game Sales with Ratings (VGChartz + Metacritic), providing Critic_Score (0-100, same scale as film Metacritic) and User_Score (0-10) per game.
Join: group_by(franchise) |> summarize(...) on each side, then left_join, giving one row per franchise.
Approach: a scatter plot of the average game score vs. the average film score per franchise, with a reference line. Points above the line are franchises whose films were better-reviewed than their games, as opposed to points below which are the opposite. I will then group the rating_gap (game score - film score) by publisher and by era to see whether certain publishers or periods show consistently larger gaps.

title: Final Project
text:
This TidyTuesday dataset, Films Based on Video Games, is scraped from the Wikipedia article List of films based on video games and curated by Georgios Karamanis. Each row is one film adaptation, with 439 films described by 20 variables and spanning releases from the mid-1980s to upcoming titles.
The data covers theatrical releases, direct-to-video, TV films, shorts, and documentaries, recording box office, budgets, critic and audience scores, distributors, and the original game publisher.
Additionally, in order to compare films against the games they are based on, I had to bring in a second dataset, Video Game Sales and Ratings (from Kaggle), which shows video games alongside their Metacritic Critic_Score (0-100, the same scale as the films’ Metacritic rating) and User_Score. This allows me to match a film’s franchise to its original game, while also giving me further insight on how each was reviewed. One limitation, however, is that the most recent data is as of 2020, which I will be addressing in Question 2.
I chose this data because I was interested in seeing how game-to-film adaptations fared over the past decade. With a rapidly growing market I wanted to see if there is any relation between the two by looking at how both have been received commercially and critically.
Source: TidyTuesday, 2026-06-09, scraped from Wikipedia’s List of films based on video games and Video Game Sales and Ratings (Kaggle).






Among films adapted from video games, how does worldwide box office vary based on the publisher of the video game that was adapted into a film and the time in which it was released, and how often do these films even break even?

Question 1 will be dealing with the commercial performance of game-to-film adaptations. In order to explore it, I had to create several columns such as release_era, profit and roi, and box_office_real built from each film’s worldwide box office, budget, source-game publisher, and release date. I also created a broke_even boolean column, so that it would be easier to show the break-even line on a scatter plot, and therefore easier to distinguish each era’s profit and loss for each adaptation. As for why I chose this question, it is because I was curious whether there is any overlap between gamers and movie enthusiasts, and if so, what that trajectory looks like. This is similar to how one might ask about books and their respective film adaptations.



The approach I took to answering my first question was by creating two different views, one faceted scatter plot and one boxplot. The first one, the faceted scatter plot was chosen deliberately, since it shows the profit and loss of each film adaptation at a glance without cluttering the view. I had initially tried an all-in-one scatter plot that showed eras by colored dots. However, it wasn’t as clear which era was more profitable than others, so I wanted to make sure this distinction is visible at a glance.
As for the second plot, the boxplot, I wanted to focus more on the publishers themselves rather than the eras the films were released in. The reason I chose the boxplot over a bar chart is that it shows each publisher’s whole distribution, the median, the spread, and any outliers. This matters and would have been lost in the bar chart, since some publishers only have a few film adaptations and one hit could easily distort the mean. Overall, I ordered the publishers by median, because it makes it immediately obvious which publishers were successful in their adaptations.






# U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics regarding Consumer Price Index (CPI)
# Source: cu.data.1.AllItems (https://download.bls.gov/pub/time.series/cu/),
# exported to data/cpi-data.csv.
cu <- read_tsv("data/cpi-data.csv", na = c("", "NA", "-")) |>   # "-" marks a withheld value
  mutate(series_id = str_trim(series_id)) |>
  filter(series_id == "CUUR0000SA0")

cpi_annual <- cu |>                          # BLS official annual average (period M13)
  filter(period == "M13") |>
  transmute(year, cpi = value)

cpi_current <- cu |>                         # current year (2026) has no annual yet:
  filter(period %in% c("M01", "M02", "M03", "M04", "M05", "M06",
                       "M07", "M08", "M09", "M10", "M11", "M12"),
         !year %in% cpi_annual$year) |>      # average its available monthly values
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(cpi = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

cpi <- bind_rows(cpi_annual, cpi_current) |> arrange(year)
cpi_base <- cpi |> filter(year == 2025) |> pull(cpi)  # choosing 2025 for most recent complete year





q1_profitability <- game_films |>
  filter(
    worldwide_box_office_currency == "$",
    !is.na(worldwide_box_office),
    !is.na(budget_low),
    budget_currency == "$"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    year = year(release_date),                                            
    release_era = factor(str_c(floor(year / 10) * 10, "s")),              # group into decades
    publisher = str_trim(str_extract(original_game_publisher, "^[^|]+")), # first studio listed (before any "|")
    publisher = fct_lump_min(publisher, min = 3),                         # keep publishers with 3+ films, rest -> "Other"
    budget_midpoint = (budget_low + budget_high) / 2,                     # budget estimate from the low-high range
    profit = worldwide_box_office - budget_midpoint,                      
    roi = worldwide_box_office / budget_midpoint,                         
    broke_even = worldwide_box_office >= budget_midpoint                  # TRUE if the film broke even or made a profit
  ) |>
  left_join(cpi, by = "year") |>                                          
  mutate(box_office_real = worldwide_box_office * cpi_base / cpi)         # adjusted for inflation as of 2025

n_profit <- nrow(q1_profitability)







# shows each film as profit (blue) or loss (red), the dashed line represents the estimated break-even point.
q1_plot_data <- q1_profitability |>
  mutate(result = factor(if_else(broke_even, "Profit", "Loss"),
                         levels = c("Profit", "Loss")))

ggplot(q1_plot_data, aes(budget_midpoint, worldwide_box_office, color = result)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +  # break-even line
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~ release_era) +
  # using log scales since budgets and grosses span several orders of magnitude ($1M to >$1B)
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  # added Set1's blue and red manually in order to keep profit on the left side
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Profit" = "#377EB8", "Loss" = "#E41A1C"), name = NULL) + 
  labs(
    title = "Most adaptations turn a profit, except in the 2000s",
    subtitle = "Worldwide box office vs. budget midpoint; the dashed line is break-even",
    x = "Production budget (midpoint)", y = "Worldwide box office"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    panel.spacing = unit(1.4, "lines"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "gray72", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "gray91", linewidth = 0.3),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray91", color = "gray72", linewidth = 0.5),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", margin = margin(4, 0, 4, 0)),
    legend.position = "top"
  )







Figure 1: Each film shown as profit (blue) or loss (red); the dashed line represents the estimated break-even point.









overall_med <- median(q1_profitability$box_office_real)

# appends each publisher's film count to its label, ordered by median box office
q1_publisher <- q1_profitability |>
  add_count(publisher, name = "pub_n") |>
  mutate(publisher_lab = fct_reorder(str_c(publisher, "  (", pub_n, " films)"),
                                     box_office_real, .fun = median))

ggplot(q1_publisher, aes(box_office_real, publisher_lab)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = overall_med, linetype = "dashed", color = "#E41A1C", linewidth = 0.7) +  # overall median
  geom_boxplot(fill = "gray84", color = "gray42", outlier.color = "gray54", width = 0.6) +
  annotate("label", x = overall_med, y = 9.2,
           label = str_c("Overall median ($", round(overall_med / 1e6), "M)"),   # tracks the base year
           size = 3, color = "#E41A1C", fill = "white", label.size = 0.4) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  scale_y_discrete(expand = expansion(add = c(0.6, 1.3))) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  labs(
    title = "Box office varies widely by source-game publisher",
    subtitle = "Publishers ordered by median worldwide box office",
    x = "Worldwide box office (inflation-adjusted to 2025 dollars)", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())







Figure 2: Each box spans the middle 50% of a publisher’s films by box office; the red line marks the overall median.










Across the 60 films, ranging from the early 1990s to present, 43 of 60 (72%) appear to have broken even or turned a profit. As can be seen in Figure 1, however, not every era is the same. Out of all the eras, 2000s was the least profitable, while the 2010s and 2020s were the most. This fits with the current industry trend where game-to-film adaptations increasingly see higher returns and have grown substantially in number. Notably, the 2020s reached this in only six years, less than a full decade, suggesting the market has not yet peaked or saturated.
As for the publisher boxplot in Figure 2, Nintendo and Sega sit at the highest, with median grosses around $440M and $420M (adj. for inflation as of 2025). This is in contrast to Konami and Sony Interactive Entertainment, which sit at or under $75M. Interestingly, Capcom seems to contribute the most in terms of adaptations while landing in the middle of the chart. One could argue that the 2000s era was less profitable due to a narrower market adoption, and that with the advent of better animation and the normalization of video games becoming part of pop culture, it would make sense that more recent eras are seeing such a surge in popularity, and therefore profit.
Last but not least, I would like to address some of the caveats I came across while wrangling the data. Since the budget is recorded as a low-high range, I had to use a midpoint, which means that if a film is at or close to break-even (the dashed line) in Figure 1, it is unclear whether it was actually profitable. Also keep in mind that several publishers, such as Eidos, Konami, and Sony, only have three film adaptations each, so the sample size may not be large enough to show a real difference. Overall, I limited the sample to the 60 films that reported both box office and budget in US dollars, and made sure to adjust all amounts for inflation as of 2025.







For video game franchises adapted into film, how do the films’ review scores compare to the ratings of their corresponding original games, and does this game-vs-film gap differ across publishers or eras?

Question 2 is focused on comparing game and film critic scores with one another. To achieve this, I had to pull in data from an outside source (Kaggle) so that I could map Metacritic scores 1:1 for both films and games. In order to do so, I had to first assign each game to its corresponding franchise, so that its score could be compared against that franchise’s film. I also derived a release_era column from the release date for era grouping, and a rating_gap column (the average game score minus the average film score) in order to measure how large the gap is and whether any trends can be observed over time.



Similar to Question 1, my approach will be centered around two different views, one being a dumbbell chart and the other a boxplot.
For the first one, I intentionally deviated from the proposed scatter plot. While the scatter plot had a clear “parity” line separating the scores for films and games, it made reading the chart difficult, as all of the game scores were clustered together on the right side of the chart because no game score was below its corresponding film score. The dumbbell chart, by contrast, not only shows the game vs. film score gap clearly, but also avoids label crowding. Lastly, sorting by gap allows people to quickly see which franchises had the widest and narrowest differences overall.
As for the second plot, the boxplot, I selected to group the rating_gap by the era of when each franchise’s first film was produced. This gave me a good indication of how the distribution of the rating_gap varied by era and if it shifted across eras. Although my original proposal suggested grouping the rating_gap by both era and publisher, since I was limited to drawing two charts from this data, I opted to go with era as it told a better story. While many publishers map to just one or two franchises, era reveals whether the game-to-film rating gap has widened or narrowed over the past decades. As a final touch, I overlaid the individual franchises as points so people viewing the chart are not deceived by the small sample size.






# Video game sales + Metacritic ratings (thedevastator, Kaggle).
# Source: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/thedevastator/video-game-sales-and-ratings
video_game_ratings <- read_csv("data/video_game_ratings.csv")





# map each title with its franchise
assign_franchise <- function(title) {
  t <- str_to_lower(title)
  case_when(
    str_detect(t, "resident evil")           ~ "Resident Evil",
    str_detect(t, "pokémon|pokemon")         ~ "Pokemon",
    str_detect(t, "mortal kombat")           ~ "Mortal Kombat",
    str_detect(t, "sonic")                   ~ "Sonic",
    str_detect(t, "street fighter")          ~ "Street Fighter",
    str_detect(t, "tomb raider|lara croft")  ~ "Tomb Raider",
    str_detect(t, "final fantasy")           ~ "Final Fantasy",
    str_detect(t, "silent hill")             ~ "Silent Hill",
    str_detect(t, "mario")                   ~ "Super Mario",
    str_detect(t, "hitman")                  ~ "Hitman",
    str_detect(t, "angry birds")             ~ "Angry Birds",
    str_detect(t, "assassin.?s creed")       ~ "Assassins Creed",
    str_detect(t, "\\bdoom\\b")              ~ "Doom",
    str_detect(t, "need for speed")          ~ "Need for Speed",
    str_detect(t, "prince of persia")        ~ "Prince of Persia",
    str_detect(t, "warcraft")                ~ "Warcraft",
    str_detect(t, "uncharted")               ~ "Uncharted",
    str_detect(t, "max payne")               ~ "Max Payne",
    str_detect(t, "alone in the dark")       ~ "Alone in the Dark",
    str_detect(t, "bloodrayne")              ~ "BloodRayne",
    str_detect(t, "double dragon")           ~ "Double Dragon",
    str_detect(t, "dead or alive")           ~ "Dead or Alive",
    str_detect(t, "house of the dead")       ~ "House of the Dead",
    str_detect(t, "ratchet")                 ~ "Ratchet & Clank",
    str_detect(t, "\\brampage\\b")           ~ "Rampage",
    str_detect(t, "monster hunter")          ~ "Monster Hunter",
    str_detect(t, "borderlands")             ~ "Borderlands",
    str_detect(t, "dungeon siege")           ~ "Dungeon Siege",
    .default = NA_character_
  )
}

# games -> one row per franchise, averaging the games' Metacritic critic scores
game_ratings <- video_game_ratings |>
  mutate(franchise = assign_franchise(Name)) |>
  filter(!is.na(franchise)) |>
  group_by(franchise) |>
  summarise(game_score = mean(Critic_Score, na.rm = TRUE), n_games = n())

# films -> one row per franchise, averaging the films' metacritic, and choosing
# when the franchise first reached the screen
film_ratings <- game_films |>
  mutate(franchise = assign_franchise(title), year = year(release_date)) |>
  filter(!is.na(franchise)) |>
  group_by(franchise) |>
  summarise(
    film_score = mean(metacritic, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_films    = n(),
    first_year = min(year, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    decade = str_c(floor(first_year / 10) * 10, "s"),
    # merging single point entry from the 1980s to keep things consistent with Question 1
    release_era = fct_recode(factor(decade),
                             "1990s and earlier" = "1980s",
                             "1990s and earlier" = "1990s")
  )

# only keep franchises present in both tables
# I decided to use an inner join rather than the left_join I had initially proposed
# since I wanted to make sure franchises without a game or film match are excluded
q2_franchise_ratings <- film_ratings |>
  inner_join(game_ratings, by = "franchise") |>
  filter(!is.nan(film_score)) |>
  mutate(rating_gap = game_score - film_score)







# reshape to long format so both score types can be drawn with one geom_point, colored by type
q2_long <- q2_franchise_ratings |>
  select(franchise, game_score, film_score, rating_gap) |>
  pivot_longer(c(game_score, film_score), names_to = "type", values_to = "score") |>
  mutate(type = if_else(type == "game_score", "Game", "Film"))

ggplot(q2_franchise_ratings, aes(y = fct_reorder(franchise, rating_gap))) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = film_score, xend = game_score,
                   yend = fct_reorder(franchise, rating_gap)),
               color = "gray72", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(data = q2_long, aes(x = score, color = type), size = 2.6) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Film" = "#E41A1C", "Game" = "#377EB8"), name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(
    title = "The game-to-film rating gap, by franchise",
    subtitle = "Each line connects a franchise's average game score to its average film score",
    x = "Critic score (Metacritic)", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top"
  )







Figure 3: Each line shows the difference between a franchise’s average game score vs. its average film score; franchises are ordered by gap size.









ggplot(q2_franchise_ratings, aes(release_era, rating_gap, fill = release_era)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.85, outlier.shape = NA, width = 0.6) +   # outliers are hidden
  geom_jitter(width = 0.12, size = 1.8, alpha = 0.6, color = "gray28") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues") +
  labs(
    title = "The game-to-film rating gap shows no consistent trend across eras",
    subtitle = "Gap = average game score minus average film score, per franchise (n = 28)",
    x = "Era of the franchise's first film", y = "Rating gap (Metacritic score)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )







Figure 4: Rating gap by the era of each franchise’s first film; boxes show the spread, gray dots the individual franchises.










As can be seen clearly in Figure 3, games out-score their film adaptations across all 28 franchises. The average gap size is around 39 Metacritic score points, although the sizes vary depending on the franchise, which can clearly be seen when comparing House of the Dead’s wide gap with Angry Birds’ narrower one. If I had to take a guess, I would say that this is due to games relying more on interactivity than cut-scene heaviness. It also does not help that most adaptations were low-budget films attached to popular game brands. With that being said, it is important to note that several of the franchises listed here only had one film or a handful of games to draw upon, so there might not be enough data to substantiate my speculation.
Now, as to whether the gap has changed over time (by era), I was not able to draw a definitive conclusion when looking at Figure 4. While it is interesting to see the median gap moving up and down between eras, rather than steadily widening or narrowing, I unfortunately have to say that there is no consistent trend overall. This poses an interesting question, however, and would need further analysis, but one might draw the conclusion that while games have out-reviewed their films fairly consistently, it did not halt the trend of seeing more game-to-film adaptations over the past decades. Does that mean review scores matter less for a game-to-film adaptation’s success than what we might assume?
Since the question is outside the scope of this project, I will instead mention a few limitations with Figure 4. One needs to keep in mind that the later eras only had a few franchises, that placing a franchise by its first film means that a long-running series is fixed to its debut era even if the films spanned decades, and that the data cutoff year is 2020. Also I would like to note that my entire premise for Question 2 was only looking at critic scores and not user scores, since the film side has no audience metric on a comparable scale. Despite that, and knowing that this would actually need a proper analysis to confirm, I did a quick analysis comparing the games’ user scores against the film critic scores, which revealed that user scores still point in the same direction, as 27 of 28 franchises favor the games, with an r value of 0.85. Last but not least, if we were to tie this back to Question 1, the gap between games and films remained consistently in the games’ favor while the industry grew at the same time, suggesting that commercial success might have been driven by other factors more than critical reception alone.

title: Final Project
text:
This TidyTuesday dataset, Films Based on Video Games, is scraped from the Wikipedia article List of films based on video games and curated by Georgios Karamanis. Each row is one film adaptation, with 439 films described by 20 variables and spanning releases from the mid-1980s to upcoming titles.
The data covers theatrical releases, direct-to-video, TV films, shorts, and documentaries, recording box office, budgets, critic and audience scores, distributors, and the original game publisher.
Additionally, in order to compare films against the games they are based on, I had to bring in a second dataset, Video Game Sales and Ratings (from Kaggle), which shows video games alongside their Metacritic Critic_Score (0-100, the same scale as the films’ Metacritic rating) and User_Score. This allows me to match a film’s franchise to its original game, while also giving me further insight on how each was reviewed. One limitation, however, is that the most recent data is as of 2020, which I will be addressing in Question 2.
I chose this data because I was interested in seeing how game-to-film adaptations fared over the past decade. With a rapidly growing market I wanted to see if there is any relation between the two by looking at how both have been received commercially and critically.
Source: TidyTuesday, 2026-06-09, scraped from Wikipedia’s List of films based on video games and Video Game Sales and Ratings (Kaggle).

title: Final Project
text:
Among films adapted from video games, how does worldwide box office vary based on the publisher of the video game that was adapted into a film and the time in which it was released, and how often do these films even break even?

Question 1 will be dealing with the commercial performance of game-to-film adaptations. In order to explore it, I had to create several columns such as release_era, profit and roi, and box_office_real built from each film’s worldwide box office, budget, source-game publisher, and release date. I also created a broke_even boolean column, so that it would be easier to show the break-even line on a scatter plot, and therefore easier to distinguish each era’s profit and loss for each adaptation. As for why I chose this question, it is because I was curious whether there is any overlap between gamers and movie enthusiasts, and if so, what that trajectory looks like. This is similar to how one might ask about books and their respective film adaptations.



The approach I took to answering my first question was by creating two different views, one faceted scatter plot and one boxplot. The first one, the faceted scatter plot was chosen deliberately, since it shows the profit and loss of each film adaptation at a glance without cluttering the view. I had initially tried an all-in-one scatter plot that showed eras by colored dots. However, it wasn’t as clear which era was more profitable than others, so I wanted to make sure this distinction is visible at a glance.
As for the second plot, the boxplot, I wanted to focus more on the publishers themselves rather than the eras the films were released in. The reason I chose the boxplot over a bar chart is that it shows each publisher’s whole distribution, the median, the spread, and any outliers. This matters and would have been lost in the bar chart, since some publishers only have a few film adaptations and one hit could easily distort the mean. Overall, I ordered the publishers by median, because it makes it immediately obvious which publishers were successful in their adaptations.






# U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics regarding Consumer Price Index (CPI)
# Source: cu.data.1.AllItems (https://download.bls.gov/pub/time.series/cu/),
# exported to data/cpi-data.csv.
cu <- read_tsv("data/cpi-data.csv", na = c("", "NA", "-")) |>   # "-" marks a withheld value
  mutate(series_id = str_trim(series_id)) |>
  filter(series_id == "CUUR0000SA0")

cpi_annual <- cu |>                          # BLS official annual average (period M13)
  filter(period == "M13") |>
  transmute(year, cpi = value)

cpi_current <- cu |>                         # current year (2026) has no annual yet:
  filter(period %in% c("M01", "M02", "M03", "M04", "M05", "M06",
                       "M07", "M08", "M09", "M10", "M11", "M12"),
         !year %in% cpi_annual$year) |>      # average its available monthly values
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(cpi = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

cpi <- bind_rows(cpi_annual, cpi_current) |> arrange(year)
cpi_base <- cpi |> filter(year == 2025) |> pull(cpi)  # choosing 2025 for most recent complete year





q1_profitability <- game_films |>
  filter(
    worldwide_box_office_currency == "$",
    !is.na(worldwide_box_office),
    !is.na(budget_low),
    budget_currency == "$"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    year = year(release_date),                                            
    release_era = factor(str_c(floor(year / 10) * 10, "s")),              # group into decades
    publisher = str_trim(str_extract(original_game_publisher, "^[^|]+")), # first studio listed (before any "|")
    publisher = fct_lump_min(publisher, min = 3),                         # keep publishers with 3+ films, rest -> "Other"
    budget_midpoint = (budget_low + budget_high) / 2,                     # budget estimate from the low-high range
    profit = worldwide_box_office - budget_midpoint,                      
    roi = worldwide_box_office / budget_midpoint,                         
    broke_even = worldwide_box_office >= budget_midpoint                  # TRUE if the film broke even or made a profit
  ) |>
  left_join(cpi, by = "year") |>                                          
  mutate(box_office_real = worldwide_box_office * cpi_base / cpi)         # adjusted for inflation as of 2025

n_profit <- nrow(q1_profitability)







# shows each film as profit (blue) or loss (red), the dashed line represents the estimated break-even point.
q1_plot_data <- q1_profitability |>
  mutate(result = factor(if_else(broke_even, "Profit", "Loss"),
                         levels = c("Profit", "Loss")))

ggplot(q1_plot_data, aes(budget_midpoint, worldwide_box_office, color = result)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +  # break-even line
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~ release_era) +
  # using log scales since budgets and grosses span several orders of magnitude ($1M to >$1B)
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  # added Set1's blue and red manually in order to keep profit on the left side
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Profit" = "#377EB8", "Loss" = "#E41A1C"), name = NULL) + 
  labs(
    title = "Most adaptations turn a profit, except in the 2000s",
    subtitle = "Worldwide box office vs. budget midpoint; the dashed line is break-even",
    x = "Production budget (midpoint)", y = "Worldwide box office"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    panel.spacing = unit(1.4, "lines"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "gray72", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "gray91", linewidth = 0.3),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray91", color = "gray72", linewidth = 0.5),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", margin = margin(4, 0, 4, 0)),
    legend.position = "top"
  )







Figure 1: Each film shown as profit (blue) or loss (red); the dashed line represents the estimated break-even point.









overall_med <- median(q1_profitability$box_office_real)

# appends each publisher's film count to its label, ordered by median box office
q1_publisher <- q1_profitability |>
  add_count(publisher, name = "pub_n") |>
  mutate(publisher_lab = fct_reorder(str_c(publisher, "  (", pub_n, " films)"),
                                     box_office_real, .fun = median))

ggplot(q1_publisher, aes(box_office_real, publisher_lab)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = overall_med, linetype = "dashed", color = "#E41A1C", linewidth = 0.7) +  # overall median
  geom_boxplot(fill = "gray84", color = "gray42", outlier.color = "gray54", width = 0.6) +
  annotate("label", x = overall_med, y = 9.2,
           label = str_c("Overall median ($", round(overall_med / 1e6), "M)"),   # tracks the base year
           size = 3, color = "#E41A1C", fill = "white", label.size = 0.4) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  scale_y_discrete(expand = expansion(add = c(0.6, 1.3))) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  labs(
    title = "Box office varies widely by source-game publisher",
    subtitle = "Publishers ordered by median worldwide box office",
    x = "Worldwide box office (inflation-adjusted to 2025 dollars)", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())







Figure 2: Each box spans the middle 50% of a publisher’s films by box office; the red line marks the overall median.










Across the 60 films, ranging from the early 1990s to present, 43 of 60 (72%) appear to have broken even or turned a profit. As can be seen in Figure 1, however, not every era is the same. Out of all the eras, 2000s was the least profitable, while the 2010s and 2020s were the most. This fits with the current industry trend where game-to-film adaptations increasingly see higher returns and have grown substantially in number. Notably, the 2020s reached this in only six years, less than a full decade, suggesting the market has not yet peaked or saturated.
As for the publisher boxplot in Figure 2, Nintendo and Sega sit at the highest, with median grosses around $440M and $420M (adj. for inflation as of 2025). This is in contrast to Konami and Sony Interactive Entertainment, which sit at or under $75M. Interestingly, Capcom seems to contribute the most in terms of adaptations while landing in the middle of the chart. One could argue that the 2000s era was less profitable due to a narrower market adoption, and that with the advent of better animation and the normalization of video games becoming part of pop culture, it would make sense that more recent eras are seeing such a surge in popularity, and therefore profit.
Last but not least, I would like to address some of the caveats I came across while wrangling the data. Since the budget is recorded as a low-high range, I had to use a midpoint, which means that if a film is at or close to break-even (the dashed line) in Figure 1, it is unclear whether it was actually profitable. Also keep in mind that several publishers, such as Eidos, Konami, and Sony, only have three film adaptations each, so the sample size may not be large enough to show a real difference. Overall, I limited the sample to the 60 films that reported both box office and budget in US dollars, and made sure to adjust all amounts for inflation as of 2025.

title: Final Project
text:
For video game franchises adapted into film, how do the films’ review scores compare to the ratings of their corresponding original games, and does this game-vs-film gap differ across publishers or eras?

Question 2 is focused on comparing game and film critic scores with one another. To achieve this, I had to pull in data from an outside source (Kaggle) so that I could map Metacritic scores 1:1 for both films and games. In order to do so, I had to first assign each game to its corresponding franchise, so that its score could be compared against that franchise’s film. I also derived a release_era column from the release date for era grouping, and a rating_gap column (the average game score minus the average film score) in order to measure how large the gap is and whether any trends can be observed over time.



Similar to Question 1, my approach will be centered around two different views, one being a dumbbell chart and the other a boxplot.
For the first one, I intentionally deviated from the proposed scatter plot. While the scatter plot had a clear “parity” line separating the scores for films and games, it made reading the chart difficult, as all of the game scores were clustered together on the right side of the chart because no game score was below its corresponding film score. The dumbbell chart, by contrast, not only shows the game vs. film score gap clearly, but also avoids label crowding. Lastly, sorting by gap allows people to quickly see which franchises had the widest and narrowest differences overall.
As for the second plot, the boxplot, I selected to group the rating_gap by the era of when each franchise’s first film was produced. This gave me a good indication of how the distribution of the rating_gap varied by era and if it shifted across eras. Although my original proposal suggested grouping the rating_gap by both era and publisher, since I was limited to drawing two charts from this data, I opted to go with era as it told a better story. While many publishers map to just one or two franchises, era reveals whether the game-to-film rating gap has widened or narrowed over the past decades. As a final touch, I overlaid the individual franchises as points so people viewing the chart are not deceived by the small sample size.






# Video game sales + Metacritic ratings (thedevastator, Kaggle).
# Source: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/thedevastator/video-game-sales-and-ratings
video_game_ratings <- read_csv("data/video_game_ratings.csv")





# map each title with its franchise
assign_franchise <- function(title) {
  t <- str_to_lower(title)
  case_when(
    str_detect(t, "resident evil")           ~ "Resident Evil",
    str_detect(t, "pokémon|pokemon")         ~ "Pokemon",
    str_detect(t, "mortal kombat")           ~ "Mortal Kombat",
    str_detect(t, "sonic")                   ~ "Sonic",
    str_detect(t, "street fighter")          ~ "Street Fighter",
    str_detect(t, "tomb raider|lara croft")  ~ "Tomb Raider",
    str_detect(t, "final fantasy")           ~ "Final Fantasy",
    str_detect(t, "silent hill")             ~ "Silent Hill",
    str_detect(t, "mario")                   ~ "Super Mario",
    str_detect(t, "hitman")                  ~ "Hitman",
    str_detect(t, "angry birds")             ~ "Angry Birds",
    str_detect(t, "assassin.?s creed")       ~ "Assassins Creed",
    str_detect(t, "\\bdoom\\b")              ~ "Doom",
    str_detect(t, "need for speed")          ~ "Need for Speed",
    str_detect(t, "prince of persia")        ~ "Prince of Persia",
    str_detect(t, "warcraft")                ~ "Warcraft",
    str_detect(t, "uncharted")               ~ "Uncharted",
    str_detect(t, "max payne")               ~ "Max Payne",
    str_detect(t, "alone in the dark")       ~ "Alone in the Dark",
    str_detect(t, "bloodrayne")              ~ "BloodRayne",
    str_detect(t, "double dragon")           ~ "Double Dragon",
    str_detect(t, "dead or alive")           ~ "Dead or Alive",
    str_detect(t, "house of the dead")       ~ "House of the Dead",
    str_detect(t, "ratchet")                 ~ "Ratchet & Clank",
    str_detect(t, "\\brampage\\b")           ~ "Rampage",
    str_detect(t, "monster hunter")          ~ "Monster Hunter",
    str_detect(t, "borderlands")             ~ "Borderlands",
    str_detect(t, "dungeon siege")           ~ "Dungeon Siege",
    .default = NA_character_
  )
}

# games -> one row per franchise, averaging the games' Metacritic critic scores
game_ratings <- video_game_ratings |>
  mutate(franchise = assign_franchise(Name)) |>
  filter(!is.na(franchise)) |>
  group_by(franchise) |>
  summarise(game_score = mean(Critic_Score, na.rm = TRUE), n_games = n())

# films -> one row per franchise, averaging the films' metacritic, and choosing
# when the franchise first reached the screen
film_ratings <- game_films |>
  mutate(franchise = assign_franchise(title), year = year(release_date)) |>
  filter(!is.na(franchise)) |>
  group_by(franchise) |>
  summarise(
    film_score = mean(metacritic, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_films    = n(),
    first_year = min(year, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    decade = str_c(floor(first_year / 10) * 10, "s"),
    # merging single point entry from the 1980s to keep things consistent with Question 1
    release_era = fct_recode(factor(decade),
                             "1990s and earlier" = "1980s",
                             "1990s and earlier" = "1990s")
  )

# only keep franchises present in both tables
# I decided to use an inner join rather than the left_join I had initially proposed
# since I wanted to make sure franchises without a game or film match are excluded
q2_franchise_ratings <- film_ratings |>
  inner_join(game_ratings, by = "franchise") |>
  filter(!is.nan(film_score)) |>
  mutate(rating_gap = game_score - film_score)







# reshape to long format so both score types can be drawn with one geom_point, colored by type
q2_long <- q2_franchise_ratings |>
  select(franchise, game_score, film_score, rating_gap) |>
  pivot_longer(c(game_score, film_score), names_to = "type", values_to = "score") |>
  mutate(type = if_else(type == "game_score", "Game", "Film"))

ggplot(q2_franchise_ratings, aes(y = fct_reorder(franchise, rating_gap))) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = film_score, xend = game_score,
                   yend = fct_reorder(franchise, rating_gap)),
               color = "gray72", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(data = q2_long, aes(x = score, color = type), size = 2.6) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Film" = "#E41A1C", "Game" = "#377EB8"), name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(
    title = "The game-to-film rating gap, by franchise",
    subtitle = "Each line connects a franchise's average game score to its average film score",
    x = "Critic score (Metacritic)", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top"
  )







Figure 3: Each line shows the difference between a franchise’s average game score vs. its average film score; franchises are ordered by gap size.









ggplot(q2_franchise_ratings, aes(release_era, rating_gap, fill = release_era)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.85, outlier.shape = NA, width = 0.6) +   # outliers are hidden
  geom_jitter(width = 0.12, size = 1.8, alpha = 0.6, color = "gray28") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues") +
  labs(
    title = "The game-to-film rating gap shows no consistent trend across eras",
    subtitle = "Gap = average game score minus average film score, per franchise (n = 28)",
    x = "Era of the franchise's first film", y = "Rating gap (Metacritic score)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )







Figure 4: Rating gap by the era of each franchise’s first film; boxes show the spread, gray dots the individual franchises.










As can be seen clearly in Figure 3, games out-score their film adaptations across all 28 franchises. The average gap size is around 39 Metacritic score points, although the sizes vary depending on the franchise, which can clearly be seen when comparing House of the Dead’s wide gap with Angry Birds’ narrower one. If I had to take a guess, I would say that this is due to games relying more on interactivity than cut-scene heaviness. It also does not help that most adaptations were low-budget films attached to popular game brands. With that being said, it is important to note that several of the franchises listed here only had one film or a handful of games to draw upon, so there might not be enough data to substantiate my speculation.
Now, as to whether the gap has changed over time (by era), I was not able to draw a definitive conclusion when looking at Figure 4. While it is interesting to see the median gap moving up and down between eras, rather than steadily widening or narrowing, I unfortunately have to say that there is no consistent trend overall. This poses an interesting question, however, and would need further analysis, but one might draw the conclusion that while games have out-reviewed their films fairly consistently, it did not halt the trend of seeing more game-to-film adaptations over the past decades. Does that mean review scores matter less for a game-to-film adaptation’s success than what we might assume?
Since the question is outside the scope of this project, I will instead mention a few limitations with Figure 4. One needs to keep in mind that the later eras only had a few franchises, that placing a franchise by its first film means that a long-running series is fixed to its debut era even if the films spanned decades, and that the data cutoff year is 2020. Also I would like to note that my entire premise for Question 2 was only looking at critic scores and not user scores, since the film side has no audience metric on a comparable scale. Despite that, and knowing that this would actually need a proper analysis to confirm, I did a quick analysis comparing the games’ user scores against the film critic scores, which revealed that user scores still point in the same direction, as 27 of 28 franchises favor the games, with an r value of 0.85. Last but not least, if we were to tie this back to Question 1, the gap between games and films remained consistently in the games’ favor while the industry grew at the same time, suggesting that commercial success might have been driven by other factors more than critical reception alone.

title: About
text:
This project was developed by [Pascal] For INFO 526 - Data Analysis and Visualization at the University of Arizona, taught by Dr. Greg Chism. The team is comprised of the following team members.

Team member 1: My name is Pascal and I am a graduate student.

title: When Games Hit the Big Screen
text:
Two datasets, two questions


The data

439 film adaptations of video games (TidyTuesday / Wikipedia) — box office, budgets, critic scores
~16,900 games with Metacritic scores (Kaggle) — to compare films against their source games


The questions

Commercial: how does box office vary by the game’s publisher and era, and how often do these films break even?
Critical: how do the films’ review scores compare to their games’ scores, and does that gap change over time/era?

title: When Games Hit the Big Screen
section: Most films profit — but not in the 2000s
text:
Most films profit — but not in the 2000s























~72% break even overall — but the 2000s dipped below half, then the 2010s-2020s recovered to 80-90%
Nintendo & Sega lead (median > $400M); publishers vary widely

title: When Games Hit the Big Screen
text:
What I tried first












A single scatter colored by era.

The overall trend is visible…
…but you can’t tell how each decade did for break-even — the colors blend together.
Faceting into one panel per decade fixed that.

title: When Games Hit the Big Screen
text:
Games always out-review their films























All 28 franchises: games score higher than their films (avg gap ~39 pts)
Across eras: no clear trend — games win consistently, whenever the franchise started

title: When Games Hit the Big Screen
text:
What I tried first












A scatter with a parity line.

Every point fell below the line, so they all bunched together…
…and the labels overlapped.
The dumbbell gave each franchise its own row and made the gap the line’s length.

title: When Games Hit the Big Screen
text:
Takeaways

Commercially, game-to-film adaptations are more profitable than ever — especially since 2010
Critically, the games still win — every franchise’s games out-scored its films

Thank you for watching! Please let me know if you have any questions.
